summary: The codebase is creative and well-intentioned, but carries several compounding correctness bugs: error-state workers are mis-classified as idle (seated at the conference table with an AwaySign), uncleared setTimeout calls leak state updates post-unmount, localStorage reads crash the entire app in Safari private mode, and Luna's SVG gradient ID duplicates when she renders twice in the same document. The vibe sparkline always lags one tick behind due to a React child-before-parent effect ordering issue. Accessibility gaps include a focusable article element with no keyboard activation, ghost characters with no accessible label, and sparkline data inaccessible to screen readers. Performance is impacted by 19 concurrent CSS animation spans when the Office tab is active. All findings are fixable with surgical changes — no architectural overhaul required.

findings.title: Error-state workers mis-classified as idle in Office.jsx
findings.description: In Office.jsx line 824: idleWorkers = nonMgr.filter(w => w.status !== 'working'). This includes workers with status === 'error' in the idle bucket. Consequences: (1) error workers get seated at a desk with the desk--idle CSS class instead of desk--error, so the red error glow and nameplate shake are never applied to their desk; (2) the AwaySign ('Coffee run', 'Deep think') appears on an erroring worker's desk after 10 minutes, actively misleading the viewer; (3) meetingWorkers is derived from idleWorkers.slice(0, 3), so an erroring worker can be seated at the conference table with a 'Huddle' badge.
findings.suggestion: Change line 824 to: const idleWorkers = nonMgr.filter(w => w.status === 'idle'). Add a separate const errorWorkers = nonMgr.filter(w => w.status === 'error'). Update desk assignment to seat error workers with ghost: false, idle: false so they get the working/error variant animations. Exclude error workers from meetingWorkers.

findings.title: Three uncleared setTimeout calls cause post-unmount state updates
findings.description: In App.jsx the activeWorkers effect (lines 826-827) calls setTimeout(() => setWorkerConfetti(null), 2500) and setTimeout(() => setCompletionToast(null), 3000) without storing the timer IDs or returning a cleanup function. Similarly, the vibe-streak effect (line 849) calls setTimeout(() => setConfettiActive(false), 2500) without cleanup. If a worker ships a task and the component unmounts within 2.5s (e.g., fast page navigation), React will log a 'state update on unmounted component' warning. In React 18 strict mode, double-invocation makes this fire reliably in dev. These bare timeouts also accumulate in production: if vibe changes twice in quick succession, the first confetti timer is orphaned with no way to cancel it.
findings.suggestion: Add confettiTimerRef and toastTimerRef refs. Store timer IDs into those refs and call clearTimeout(ref.current) before each new setTimeout. Return a cleanup function from each useEffect that calls clearTimeout(ref.current). Alternatively consolidate the two completion-related timers into a single useEffect that watches [workerConfetti, completionToast] and cleans up on dependency change.

findings.title: localStorage reads unguarded — crashes entire app in Safari private mode
findings.description: Lines 582, 835, and 836 in App.jsx call localStorage.getItem() and localStorage.setItem() without try/catch. In Safari private browsing mode and some embedded WebViews, localStorage access throws SecurityError: DOM Exception 18. Line 582 runs inside a useState initializer — a synchronous throw here propagates out of the component render and crashes the entire app with a blank white screen. The vibe-history block (lines 854-858) correctly wraps in try/catch, but the streak reads and theme initialization do not. This is a real bug for any user in private mode.
findings.suggestion: Create a utils/storage.js with: function safeGet(key, fallback) { try { return localStorage.getItem(key) ?? fallback } catch { return fallback } } and function safeSet(key, value) { try { localStorage.setItem(key, value) } catch {} }. Replace all 8 bare localStorage calls in App.jsx with these helpers. The useState initializer on line 582 is highest priority as it is the only synchronous throw path.

findings.title: Luna's SVG linearGradient ID is always 'luna-hair-luna' — breaks when rendered twice
findings.description: In Office.jsx line 252: const gradId = 'luna-hair-' + workerId. Since workerId is always the string 'luna', this always produces the ID 'luna-hair-luna'. CharacterAvatar renders Luna both via Character at the desk and via CharacterAvatar inside ConferenceTable's seated avatars when meetingWorkers includes Luna. SVG id attributes must be unique per document. When duplicated, browsers use whichever linearGradient appears last in the DOM, making Luna's hair in the first instance resolve to black or transparent.
findings.suggestion: Use React 18's useId() hook inside CharacterAvatar to generate a stable unique ID per instance: const uid = useId(); const gradId = 'luna-hair-' + uid. This ensures each rendered instance gets its own gradient definition regardless of how many times CharacterAvatar mounts.

findings.title: StatsBar reads vibe history from localStorage before App writes it — sparkline always one tick stale
findings.description: StatsBar's useEffect (App.jsx lines 393-405) reads VIBE_HISTORY_KEY from localStorage and sets vibeHistory state. It depends on [vibe.key]. In the same render cycle, App's vibe-streak effect (lines 833-860) writes a new entry to VIBE_HISTORY_KEY — also triggered by [teamVibe.key]. React runs child effects before parent effects, so StatsBar's read fires before App's write. Result: the sparkline always shows the history from the previous vibe change. On a stable vibe (e.g., 'crushing' for 30 minutes), the sparkline never updates even though new entries are being written.
findings.suggestion: Extract a useVibeHistory(vibeKey) custom hook that owns both the write (on vibe change) and the read (for display). Call it once in App, pass the resulting history array down as a prop to StatsBar. This eliminates the parent-child effect ordering race by keeping read and write in the same hook scope.

findings.title: WorkerCard uses role='article' with tabIndex=0 — articles must not be focusable interactive elements
findings.description: The WorkerCard outer div (App.jsx line 249) has role='article' and tabIndex={0}. The ARIA 'article' role is structural, not interactive — it should never appear in the tab order. Screen readers announce it as 'article' when focused, but Tab + Enter/Space does nothing (no onKeyDown handler on the outer div). Meanwhile, the info button and task tag spans inside the card are also separately focusable, creating a confusing two-stop focus sequence per card. This violates WCAG 2.1 SC 4.1.2: the element advertises an interactive affordance it does not provide.
findings.suggestion: Remove tabIndex={0} and role='article' from the outer div. Replace with role='group' and aria-labelledby pointing to the worker name element. This keeps the card as a labelled group without putting it in the tab order. The internal button elements already provide all necessary keyboard interaction.

findings.title: Ghost character divs have tabIndex=0 but no accessible role or label
findings.description: In Office.jsx the Character component (line 490) always renders with tabIndex={0}. For ghost variants, the conditional role={handleClick ? 'button' : undefined} evaluates to undefined since ghost characters have no onClick. The ghost OOO bubble is only revealed via onFocus which calls handleMouseEnter, but the containing div has no role or aria-label — a keyboard user focusing it hears nothing useful. The data-tooltip attribute is also explicitly suppressed for ghosts. Screen reader users cannot identify who is OOO or why.
findings.suggestion: For ghost characters, add role='img' and aria-label={'worker.name + ' is out of office, ' + worker.role} to the outer div. Move the OOO bubble text into a visually-hidden sibling span so screen readers can read it without hover interaction. If ghosts should not be keyboard-focusable at all, set tabIndex={variant === 'ghost' ? -1 : 0}.

findings.title: CompletionToast and ShortcutToast overlap — toast overflows on mobile with long names
findings.description: The completionToast element (App.jsx lines 941-965) uses inline styles with whiteSpace: 'nowrap' and no maxWidth constraint. On a 375px mobile viewport, names longer than ~22 characters overflow beyond the screen edge. Both ShortcutToast and completionToast render at position fixed, bottom: 2rem — when a worker ships a task within 7 seconds of the page loading, both toasts render at exactly the same position and overlap completely. The inline style object is also recreated on every render, bypassing shallow comparison optimizations.
findings.suggestion: Move the completion toast styles to App.css as .completion-toast with max-width: calc(100vw - 2rem) and white-space: normal. Give ShortcutToast a bottom: 1rem and completionToast a bottom: 5rem so they stack vertically instead of overlapping. Use a CSS class instead of inline styles to avoid per-render object allocation.

findings.title: getRelativeTime is duplicated verbatim in Office.jsx ghost bubble — will drift
findings.description: App.jsx defines getRelativeTime(timestamp) at line 28 with identical logic. Office.jsx contains a character-for-character copy as an inline IIFE inside the ghost bubble render (lines 505-513): same variable names (diff, mins, hours, days), same return strings ('just now', mins + 'm ago', etc.). This is a classic DRY violation — if formatting logic changes (e.g., adding a 'seconds' granularity or localization), one copy will be missed. This function should live in utils/time.js alongside the other utilities.
findings.suggestion: Create src/utils/time.js exporting getRelativeTime(timestamp) and formatFullDateTime(timestamp). Remove both inline definitions from App.jsx and Office.jsx and replace with imports. While in this file, also extract formatIdleDuration (Office.jsx line 318) and formatDuration (App.jsx line 215) which share identical rounding logic.

findings.title: 19 concurrent CSS animations when Office tab is active (dual ambient-particle sets)
findings.description: App.jsx (line 918) renders 12 ambient-particle spans with CSS keyframe animations, always in the DOM. Office.jsx (line 1149) renders an additional 7 ambient-particle spans with explicit animationDuration and animationDelay inline styles. When the Office tab is active, all 19 spans animate simultaneously alongside desk entry animations, character bob/wander animations, the vibe shockwave, typing dots (3 per working character), and the walking dustmotes. The Office.jsx particles use the same CSS class name 'ambient-particle' as App.jsx, but the Office floor is position: relative with overflow hidden — these particles may be clipped unexpectedly without providing value.
findings.suggestion: Remove the 7 duplicate ambient-particle spans from Office.jsx (lines 1149-1158). App.jsx's 12 particles already provide atmospheric ambience at the app level with data-vibe driving their color theme. If a distinct floor-level atmosphere is needed, create a separate .office-floor-particle class with position: absolute and clip to the floor container. Ensure the prefers-reduced-motion block in App.css targets this class explicitly.

findings.title: VibeSparkline data not accessible — SVG circle titles are not reliably read by screen readers
findings.description: The VibeSparkline SVG (App.jsx line 353) has aria-label='Vibe history' on the root element, but the data encoded in the sparkline (up to 12 vibe transitions with timestamps) is only accessible via SVG title elements inside each circle. SVG title elements have poor, inconsistent cross-browser screen reader support — Firefox + NVDA ignores them unless the shape has role='img' or aria-labelledby. A user navigating by keyboard or screen reader cannot discover the team's vibe trajectory, which is core dashboard information.
findings.suggestion: Add a visually-hidden text summary adjacent to the SVG: a span with className='sr-only' containing 'Vibe history: ' + history.map(e => e.key + ' at ' + new Date(e.ts).toLocaleTimeString()).join(', '). Replace the SVG's aria-label with aria-describedby pointing to that span. This approach works universally across assistive technology without relying on SVG title support.

findings.title: No immediate re-sync after returning from idle — data can be up to 60 minutes stale
findings.description: The idle detection pauses polling after 10 minutes of no mouse/keyboard activity. When the user returns, the next poll fires at the end of the current interval (up to 30 seconds later). Since they could have been idle for up to 30 minutes, data displayed can be 30 + 30 = 60 minutes old before refreshing. The lastActivity.current update in the mousemove handler (line 625) only updates the timestamp that pollTick checks — it does not trigger an immediate sync. There is no 'activity resumed' event equivalent to visibilitychange.
findings.suggestion: Track a wasIdleRef = useRef(false). In pollTick, when Date.now() - lastActivity.current > IDLE_TIMEOUT, set wasIdleRef.current = true. In the mousemove/keydown/touchstart handler, check if wasIdleRef.current is true and isSyncing is false, then call syncFromGitHub() immediately and set wasIdleRef.current = false. This ensures the user sees fresh data within one interaction of returning from an idle period.
